claim: Always serialize timestamps in UTC to avoid cross-region comparison bugs
rationale: prior incidents with mixed local/UTC timestamps in order ledger

claim: Include at least one dreamer teammate in non-trivial agent teams
rationale: Dreamer teammates fill idle time with state maintenance, surfacing decisions and updates that would otherwise be lost. Validated across multiple agent-team runs in schrodan.

claim: Team lead delegates aggressively — opus architects/plans/reviews, never reads or writes code or state files directly
rationale: Lead model spent on implementation work duplicates capacity that subagents already have. Lead must remain in orchestration mode to make sequencing decisions.

claim: Implementation teammates work on branches with isolation: worktree; only the lead or user merges to main
rationale: Without worktree isolation, teammates share working tree and can commit to main, add unrequested files, or modify state. Incident: add-qwen team 2026-04-21 had to be cherry-picked and reverted.

claim: Implementation teammates report state changes; only dreamers write .dreams/ and .memories/ files
rationale: Separation prevents implementation agents from accidentally rewriting project-state files mid-task. Dreamers consume reports and update state in their own pass.

claim: Temp files for inter-agent context sharing belong under .memories/tmp/, never /tmp/ or .scratch/
rationale: Repo-local temp dirs survive sandbox restrictions and are visible to other agents. /tmp/ is volatile and may be inaccessible under macOS Seatbelt.

claim: For changes under 50 lines, use a single gemma4:26b call and skip the planning model
rationale: Two-model pipelines (plan + implement) added 10s latency and 800 tokens for a 20-line change in schrodan benchmarks. The planning model's overhead exceeded the value it added at small task sizes.

claim: Embed exact type signatures and struct definitions in local-model prompts, not descriptions
rationale: qwen3-coder rewrote build_tree() signature it hadn't seen, hallucinating fields that didn't exist. Local models infer poorly from descriptions; concrete code anchors them.

claim: Request diff-format output from local code models for edits to existing files
rationale: Full-file output wastes 1500+ tokens reproducing unchanged code. Diff format is compact, focused, and easier for the orchestrator to apply.

claim: Orchestrators must strip markdown code fences from local-model output — every model adds them despite instructions
rationale: qwen3-coder, gemma4, and qwen3.6 all add ```rust / ``` fences regardless of system prompt instructions to omit them. Strip in the orchestrator instead of fighting it per-model.

claim: Orchestrators should fix trivial compile errors directly rather than re-dispatching to a local model
rationale: Type mismatches and missing imports cost <5s of orchestrator time but trigger another 20-30s round-trip if re-dispatched. Re-dispatch only for substantive logic changes.

claim: Never run parallel writes to the same file from multiple subagents
rationale: lib.rs mod declarations from concurrent agents produce duplicate definitions that compile-break. Sequence file writes or partition by file.

claim: Use stdin piping (cat | gemini --yolo) for multi-line gemini prompts; nix run wrapper mangles them
rationale: nix run .#gemini -- -p '$prompt' loses newlines via shell quoting. Pipe a .scratch/prompt.md file instead and clean up after.

claim: Don't dispatch to gemini for review-only tasks — gemini may edit files unprompted
rationale: gemini's --yolo flag auto-approves writes. For read-only review use sonnet/haiku or local models. Use gemini only for tasks where file writes are expected.

claim: Don't dispatch to gemini when opus already has full task context loaded
rationale: Round-trip to gemini wastes wall-clock time when opus could write the change directly with the context already in its window.

claim: Before marking a team's final task complete, ask 'What did this team learn that isn't in existing memories?' and save non-obvious findings
rationale: Without an explicit close-down step, novel insights surface during the work but never get persisted. Incident: model-audit team produced findings that would have been lost without prompting.

claim: Haiku subagents have a summarization bias — instruct them to 'Return exact content verbatim, do not summarize or paraphrase'
rationale: Without explicit instruction, haiku will paraphrase content even when fidelity is required (e.g., copying error messages, code snippets). Affects every haiku dispatch where exact text matters.

claim: Haiku subagents may write files unsolicited — instruct them 'Do not create, write, or modify any files' for read-only tasks
rationale: haiku has shown unsanctioned-write behavior on read-only research tasks. Negative constraint at the start of the prompt is the reliable mitigation.

claim: Keep haiku prompts under 200 words — prompt length insensitivity degrades response quality
rationale: haiku's adherence to instructions drops with prompt length. Above ~200 words it begins ignoring constraints. Use sonnet for tasks needing rich context.

claim: Don't fan out to 4+ parallel haiku subagents for tasks under 100 tokens of work each — overhead dominates
rationale: Each haiku subagent has 25-30k tokens of overhead. 4 parallel haikus on small tasks = 112k+ tokens spent on dispatch overhead alone. Inline the work in the lead instead.

claim: Sonnet subagents over-investigate by default — scope searches explicitly: 'Read file X, report fields Y and Z'
rationale: Without explicit scoping, sonnet will read adjacent files, follow imports, and produce a longer result than needed. Cap the search surface in the prompt.
